Any chance I could get a nickel tour of this week’s Robert Fludd plate?
Thank you.
Rhydlyd

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's a cosmological diagram. The uppermost circle is the divine, which is divided into a bright and a dark half -- in Fludd's terminology, the Bright Aleph and the Dark Aleph, which are the two aspects of the divine. The Bright Aleph manifests itself in our cosmos as the Sun, the Dark Aleph as the Earth -- think Solar Logos and Planetary Spirit. The two half circles down below are commentary: the figure in the dark semicircle is Dionysus, who is tearing someone apart; the figure in the bright semicircle is Apollo, who has put the person back together and healed them.  All in all, the diagram is good for about a month of solid meditation.

comments.title: My experience with the Chenrezig mantra as a non Buddhist, aka Good Is Not Nice
comments.content:
I just thought I should report on my experiences as a non-Buddhist chanting the Om Mani Padme Hum mantra.

After having visited a Buddhist temple, I'd started chanting Om Mani Padme Hum, and eventually, as I have had experience with the Middle Pillar exercise I realised that each syllable belonged to an energy center. I placed Om at crown of head, Ma at center of forehead, Ni at throat, Pad at Heart, Me at groin, and Hum at feet.

As a result, I found that I could not digest my meal and was violently ill. Next day was a full day of fasting, and the rest of the week was forced vegetarianism. I also, on a whim, gave a beggar much more money than he asked, enough for the packet of cigarettes he clearly wanted.

So... TSW, but at a cost. I think I'd need to have a think and do some divinations as to whether continuing that is a good idea.

replies.content: Er, well, yes. This does not sound like a good idea to me.

replies.content:
As a practicing Buddhist - and someone who likes reading this blog - it seems that the intermixing of Buddhist practice within non Buddhist spiritual paths should only be done with the greatest of care and should be immediately abandoned upon a negative result.

We do a LOT of what I would call the equivalent of banishing spells as a daily practice and a LOT of study on the nature of Karma. After 30 years of Dharma practice I still feel like a beginner a lot of the times.

Its hardcore Mahāyāna and comes with a TON of guidance that amounts to right thought and right action - a lot of "do this" and "dont do this"

replies.title: Re: My experience with the Chenrezig mantra as a non Buddhist, aka Good Is Not Nice
replies.content:
In Tibetan Buddhism, we’ve been taught that the syllable OM, at the forehead, represents the body of all the Buddhas; the syllable AH, at the throat, represents the speech of all the Buddhas; the syllable HUM, at the heart, represents the mind of all the Buddhas. This imagery is often used in visualizations, pictures of sacred beings, etc. There are additional chakras, but they would not usually be discussed with beginning students. I have never heard the feet considered as an energy center in Tibetan Buddhism.

I’ve never heard of the syllables of the Om Mani Peme Hum mantra being paired with specific energy centers. I just did a quick Google search and didn’t find any examples of this.

Since it is a six syllable mantra, there are many teachings explaining how the six syllables purify the six negative emotions and support the six perfections. If you look at the Wikipedia entry on the mantra,
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Om_mani_padme_hum#In_Tibetan_Buddhism
you’ll find a chart about this, plus teachings on the mantra from three respected teachers with links to a great deal more material.

JMG has written books on several different paths of practice. My impression, from my few months visiting ecosophia, is that he emphasizes doing a path fully and in order. He is very careful about saying which paths go together and which do not.

The mantra Om Mane Padme Hum invokes the Buddha of compassion; it is a way of increasing our own ability to experience and express compassion. Compassion is the beginning and the heart of the Buddhist path. Therefore I think chanting this mantra, or listening to the chant, with those intentions, is a valuable and safe practice for everyone.

The Buddhist path starts with meditation and compassion. It goes on to additional practices, complex visualizations, etc. In my experience, it is a wonderful blessing to learn practices that have been created over centuries and practiced by thousands of goodhearted people. But the safest way of following the Buddhist path (like any other path) is to practice it in order and when possible under the guidance of a teacher.

Best wishes for your future practice! May everything be auspicious for you, on the physical plane and the spiritual plane!

EllenZ

replies.title: Re: My experience with the Chenrezig mantra as a non Buddhist, aka Good Is Not Nice
replies.content: Valuable? Maybe. Safe? No. Surely anyone who's been a fool in the name of compassion — and I am one — is going to recommend a touch of caution about flinging oneself wholeheartedly into a compassion practice, and double caution about goosing it up. If you're lucky, you only wind up giving more money to a beggar than you'd otherwise consider appropriate. If you're not lucky, you'll tangle with someone who is both vulnerable and exploitative/destructive, and you'll get a much more expensive lesson in "idiot compassion".

replies.content: True. But it does make sense if you are within a Sangha and you follow of the school teachings about interpersonal relationships. Most safeguards are rules set by the school.

comments.content:
JMG,

1. Some occult writers on the GD system state that you don’t have to begin and end the Middle Pillar exercise with the Qabalistic Cross because the MP itself is a “super-charged” form of the QC. Do you agree with this claim?

2. Does the occult model of the universe imply that there are no ‘miracles’ as popularly imagined in the major religions? Manifestation follows the path of Creation, from the pure will of the divine, through the mental and astral realms, into the etheric and material planes. To the extent that the divine will manifests, it occurs through synchronicity: apparently meaningful coincidences following the laws of nature on the material plane, and not through a suspension of the natural order. The magician, in turn, influences the process of manifestation by formulating symbolic patterns on the astral plane and allowing them to descend into the etheric and material planes. What is your personal stance on miracles?

3. It seems that the occult view of spirits implies that they have no physical bodies, and that our interaction with them is limited to the astral and etheric planes, as phenomena in consciousness. Is the depiction of angels, demons, and other supernatural entities in the major world religions as embodied entities an example of telesmatic imagery? Is it possible to ever “see” such an entity in 3-dimensional space?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Not at all. The Cabalistic Cross has a different energetic structure than the MP exercise, and both of them are useful. As Israel Regardie points out in one of his books, the Cabalistic Cross includes a great many potentials which are not at all apparent to a casual glance.

2) We have no idea whether there is a fixed natural order, and if there is one, we have only the sketchiest idea what it might be. (For all we know, nature has habits rather than laws...) Thus the entire conventional concept of a miracle is handwaving. In our time it's apparently quite rare for superhuman beings to intervene on the material plane in unsubtle ways, and claims that this has happened need very good evidence to support them, but I'd be silly to insist they can't happen.

3) Of course. The etheric plane is subject to space and time, and the astral plane can be.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Would I be correct in speculating that on the mental plane is beyond time in space, not in the way that the spiritual plane is, but in the sense that time and space are two "residents" of the plane rather than part of the structure of the plane itself?

I get this idea from models of computation. Ordinary computers approximate the Turing machine model, which has built-in conceptions of time and space: the computer executes a sequence of instructions (time), and uses some form of memory for intermediate results (space). But there are other models of computation that make no such assumptions: for them, time and space have to defined in terms of more basic objects.

(What's really fascinating is that these models are all exactly equivalent in what can be expressed by them.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I strongly suggest making your own. The feeling of "too big for you" is a test. You will learn much more by passing it than you will from spending 50 bucks online.

I made my own magical working tools when I was living in a slum town in Central California making about $100 per week if I was lucky. The pentacle was made from a wooden disk that cost about $1 at Michael's, with 4 $1.50 bottles of paint and a good pen (another couple dollars) to write the Hebrew characters. The chalice was a blue goblet I found at the local Good Will; if you can't find a blue goblet, you can buy a cheap wineglass and paint it blue, then paint orange over the blue-- again, paints are about a buck apiece. The dagger was a plain dagger that cost about $15 on Amazon, and was the most expensive item; again, after that you just need yellow and purple paint. The wand, finally, was an oddly-shaped dowel rod that cost a dollar or two at a craft store.

Making magical tools is always challenging, but the challenge isn't a problem for your magical development, it's part of it. Do them one at a time, do what you can, and don't expect them to be perfect. I promise you will not regret it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, but in Nietzsche's case the connection is a little complex. He was gay -- it's astonishing how much effort his biographers have had to put into obscuring that very obvious point -- and he never got over the crisis early in his college years when he discovered his sexuality. It used to be a very common story: a sensitive and devoutly Christian young person, who's been taught that same-sex relationships are always and only the most debased form of lust, falling head over heels in love with a person of the same sex and having to deal with the conflict between the nasty moral slanders of his religious background and the discovery that romantic love, in the highest and richest sense of the word, also happens between people of the same sex. Such people usually become atheists of an unusually bitter type. (I'm pretty sure that's what happened with James Randi, for example.) Nietzsche's problem is that he remained powerfully attracted to his former Christian faith; he turned his life into a war against God as a reaction against that attraction, and the internal conflict finally shattered his mind. Read his late books, as his tone gets more and more shrill, and you can see the conflict build to the breaking point.

replies.title: erasing gayness
replies.content:
This applies mainly to literary figures--the school of criticism popular in middle of the 20th century, known as the New Criticism, emphasized close reading of the text, analyzing such linguistic features as metaphor, simile, imagery, paradox, ambiguity. Social aspects of literature, including the biographical facts of the author's life were downplayed. I suspect that one reason for its popularity in the US was that it avoided Marxism during the era when being suspected of socialist leanings could doom an academic career. In any case, this type of criticism made the sexual life, or the occult life, of the author irrelevant. This made it attractive for those who did not want 'warts and all' treatment of their literary heroes, whether the wart was homosexuality, alcohol or drug abuse, rampant womanizing, unpopular political or social beliefs, unacceptable religion, etc. OTH there is still a large number of people who regard homosexuality as a sin, or a sickness, and who do not wish to be confronted with this "failing" in an author they like. I had a professor who promised to discuss the sexuality of Henry James, but somehow never got around to it, and this was in 1991, well into the time of modern attitudes. On the third hand there are the homosexual activists who wish to "recruit" the famous dead, sometimes on the slimmest of evidence.

Rita

comments.title: Ghosts, Slime, and Schizophrenia
comments.content:
Mr. Greer --

This is going to be a long one, unfortunately.

In Fall 2019, I moved into my college dorm. This college is built on the grounds (and using the buildings) of an asylum that began in 1933. Most of the old buildings have been updated and renovated into classrooms and offices, except for four. One is next to the bus stop, and I thought it was still in use for offices.

When I came up towards the entrance, black tendrils appeared from the door in my secondary vision. It was as though there was this black slime coming out of it, and I finally got close enough to read the sign on the door, CHILDREN'S PSYCHIATRIC WARD. I backed up, bowing and apologizing, because I could feel so much energy coming off of it, angry and hungry, and I ran off. I thought I could cut through a courtyard, but it was a dead end, and the children's ward was on all sides. It felt like there were hundreds of eyes watching me from the windows. I could feel what they wanted, for me to come into the building with them, and just as surely, I knew that if I went in, I would never come out.

I already had my statuette of Pan in my pocket and my silver amulet around my neck, but there was a piece of rebar at my feet, and I grabbed that too. I said I didn't want any trouble, I would never come back, or bring anybody, that I just wanted to get out of there. I backed out of the courtyard, and left the rebar at the entrance in case it was needed by anyone else.

I started carrying a chain of additional amulets around as the semester wore on. There were other incidents like this, and eventually it was decided my medication should be increased. I wasn't scared so much after that. I've been on antipsychotics and other medications for years, but I've often wondered whether the incident in the courtyard could be classified as something occult in nature. If so, what happened? Is there a better way to handle this sort of thing in the future? Besides remembering where the courtyard entrances are, of course.

As for how to handle it, since you're on psychiatric meds, I don't recommend practicing ritual magic; thus avoidance is probably your best bet.

There is a really strong left-hand path (western sense, not vamachara) streak to his work and his Rune Gild really put me off when I looked at them. He suffers some criticism for not being "historically accurate" which really varies by issue. He has the habit of transcribing larger trends in german occultism into his runic system to fill in missing material from the original sources. I really like that approach but a naive reading tends to lead to an accusation of making stuff up. He's an inescapable major figure, that's why I recommend him.
The particular runic system and alphabet are partially independent. Younger futhark is the one used by the vikings and is the subject of most of the historical inscriptions, but it also feels very incomplete to me. The elder futhark has a smaller corpus but it interfaces in more obvious ways with both natural patterns and with the older underlying indo-european religious tradition. It probably isn't a coincidence that the later latin, greek, and egyptian writing systems all had 24 characters as well. Most books on the subject deal with this one. The anglo-saxon futhorc (not a typo) has 33 characters and is the topic of the most comprehensive of the actually extant rune poems. The added runes also deal nicely with Arthurian themes (ie. stone, spear, and chalice). I would be willing to bet money that it could be cleanly interfaced with druidry though that isn't a project I've seen anyone attempt. Alaric Albertsson's book on the subject is good history with a very fluffy approach to spirituality. Finally, the Armanen Runes are associated (somewhat wrongly) with Nazism. They are a pretty recent invention and since my interest was more history and theory than practice, I only gave them a cursory examination.
On modern inscriptions, elder futhark seems to be the most common on printed viking-themed media despite not being historical (it should be the younger futhark). I suspect this is a result of the popularity of occult books dealing with the elder futhark. Also, the blank or "wyrd" rune is a modern invention; it doesn't exist in any system prior to the 70's when a man named Ralph Blum released a book on the subject. Some people have been able to get his system to work. The divinatory meanings weren't recorded and thus aren't standardized, so you'll see a lot less consistency in readings between practitioners than with a system like Tarot or Geomancy.

replies.title: Re: Mundane Astrology/Lunar Eclipse
replies.content: Nyet! Viruses are like chain letters, which can't reproduce themselves and rely on their host to reproduce. Errors in this process (like if you mess up while copying the chain letter) create variants. The more people you send the letter to, and the more copies each of them makes, the more variants there will be - most of which will be "inferior", but some of which might be "improvements" on the original. Likewise, more hosts having no pre-existing resistance means more virus variants. If this guy wants us to think, contrarily, that having a lot of people in the population who won't get infected because they have prior immunity is Bad, isn't it also Bad to have lots of people get sick because then they too will be immune afterward (except for those who die, natch)?

replies.title: Re: Mundane Astrology/Lunar Eclipse
replies.content: Not the OP, but the problem is that the vaccines only provide partial immunity. In much the same way that bacteria evolve to resist antibiotics which are overused, an emphasis on immunity to a single protein will encourage the virus to evolve to find ways around that. Most people's immune responses are not based on a single protein, but on plenty of them, with some idiosyncratic variation thrown in just for fun as well. This makes it harder for the virus to get around the immune response than just using a vaccine.

replies.title: Re: Mundane Astrology/Lunar Eclipse
replies.content:
I'm not convinced by that argument. Mutations, like copying errors, are random. Most vaccinated people aren't going to get infected, so those who do will generate some variants, but fewer of them than if ten times as many people got infected and produced copies. If variant B is much more able to infect vaccinated people than variant A, then in a heavily vaccinated population variant B, and variants based on it, will become dominant. But how is that any worse than not vaccinating, and having everyone get sick with variant A? (It could be worse if variant B is also more virulent, but the opposite could just as easily be true.)

Second, all the emphasis is on production of antibodies to the protein because - or so I get the impression - that's what it's cheapest to test for, but the immune response to vaccines, like infections, apparently does also include production of T cells, which may go after cells that are infected even with variants that the antibodies don't recognize well.

comments.title: Is Permaculture an Alchemy ?
comments.content:
Dear John,

I have been meditating upon why appropriate technologies and Permaculture were not widely adopted by those who claim to favour a green lifestyle.

Your writings about Alchemy and Alchemies may be a useful part of the puzzle.

I came to discover Permaculture at the same time as appropriate technology, around the late 1970’s. There was a TV show about Bill Mollison and he described the system in an Australian context. In simple terms it is the use of mutually supportive biological systems in an organized manner. The patterns and flows of wind, water and energy are all tracked and incorporated. Examples include trees for shelter, crops and no-dig systems to protect soil structure.

At the same time I was reading Taoist literature and exploring Anarchist politics. Both sets of ideas talk of mutual aid and working with the flow of nature. I think these primed my mind as it were.

As I re-read "Permaculture One", I became aware that he had been influenced by Taoism and Eastern systems. Is this why Permaculture has not been adopted more widely in the West ?

However, there are many books and internet pages that use the Permacultre ideas, but in a more watered down way, losing the underlying philosophical content. Not to mention the parasitical social justice content that derails the focus in many books and on most Permaculture forums.

Then there is the excessive emphasis placed on "doing the course", which is simply teaching the text of the Mollison books.

So this reminded me of your Alchemy and “Alchemies” discussion. I think that maybe the Alchemy of Permaculture depends on the Taoist pattern / flow metaphor being suitably embedded. Certain philosophical ideas need to be constructed as a foundation for certain Alchemies to work. Thus the changing of consciousness, the magic, requires certain ingredients for the ritual to work.

Sorry if I rambled somewhat, but I hope this may be of interest.

Thank you again for this forum. There seems to be nowhere else I can share ideas of this sort.

Best regards Northern Ben

replies.title: Re: Is Permaculture an Alchemy ?
replies.content:
Good. Yes, exactly -- permaculture in its full form depends on an alchemical philosophy, and if you take that away, all you've got left is a grab bag of landscaping hints. Of course people want to do that, because it allows them to remain comfortably stuck in the rut of modern pop-culture consciousness.

Interestingly, the same thing is true of biodynamic gardening. That depends on Western occult philosophy -- it was launched by Rudolf Steiner -- and can be debased just as readily, though even in debased form you can still get decent vegetable harvests.

I've become repelled by the virtual entertainment industry and by all forms of advertising, and as a result I got rid of my TV and stopped consuming most forms of online content.

I’m also pleased to report that I’ve completely transformed my life for the better in the past 1.5 years. I found a new group of friends that are extremely supportive of my life goals, found an incredible girlfriend, moved into a new place, and changed jobs for something more suited to my personality and interests. I’d like to attribute all of this to coincidence, but it seems strangely suspicious that it overlaps so well with my taking up of magic.

When I was first getting into magic, I was expecting big transformative moments, but instead the change has been very gradual and subtle, with the rituals gaining power and unfolding their meaning over months of practice. Is this a typical experience, and can I expect it to continue?

comments.title: A very serious question about karma
comments.content:
Do the more advanced* animals like primates incur a karmic burden for behaviors like (to pick one I personally find reprehensible) randomly battering subordinates to insure their own dominance? Or does that not matter because they are innocent animals acting according to their nature? *I do realize you can't fault a cat for playing with a mouse it's caught!*

And what about humans living in a culture where such behaviors are not only the norm, but considered right and proper. What karmic burden do they incur? We postmoderns have gone on a crusade to condemn people of the past for behaving in ways we of the future dislike, and am I projecting that when I ask this question? Even when the behaviors visibly do harm to others? That famous "Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do," keeps coming to mind.

And if any of this is true, then the standards we are supposed to live up to are those of angels, and as one of Shakespeare's characters said, "then no man would escape a whipping."

Or is this just a formerly Christian background finding this to be a problem?

Thanks,

The Grey Badger

replies.title: Re: A very serious question about karma
replies.content:
As I understand it, all human souls incur karma for everything they do, good, indifferent, and bad. So do animals -- the cat who plays with a mouse is helping to build the character of the soul incarnate in it, by taking on the karma of innocent cruelty. The crucial point to remember here is that karma is not blame. Karma is simply the consequences of action. We all inevitably generate it, and experience its consequences, life after life, as an inescapable part of learning the lessons of the human plane. By the time a soul on the human level has reached the point of assessing actions on the basis of their karmic results, that soul has come a long way and is preparing to move on beyond the human level.

Karma is not blame, and nobody gets punished for it; we simply experience the results of our actions, and learn from them.

replies.title: Re: A very serious question about karma
replies.content:

What sort(s) of karma do our actions towards animals (or plants, for that matter) generate?

For instance, I was reading a kid's book on Jane Goodall. It makes mention of infant chimpanzees being kidnapped and sold to laboratories, which made me tear up. Does egregious cruelty towards less-developed souls generate less karma than egregious cruelty towards humans?

replies.title: Re: A very serious question about karma

comments.title: Concurrently Practicing Multiple Religions
comments.content:
1. Would a full Moon be the best time to place a silver pendant outside to create a protective amulet?

2. The last couple of months, the full Moon occurred on a rainy night in my area. Would all that cold water falling down on the intended amulet prevent it from charging? If so, would placing the pendant in a clear plastic bag or container allow it to absorb the energy of the Moon while rain is falling?

3. Would practicing several religions concurrently be a bad idea? I have taken up the practice of Catholic sacramental magic but I have also recently created an altar to the Vodou lwa and have been serving them, with positive improvements in my life as a result. I have also put together a shrine for the Roman Deities and I will try to venerate and honor them via the praxis of Religio Romana. I devote specific days of the week and month to each religious path, so that I don’t end up overwhelmed by ritual work. Of course, Catholicism and Vodou were, and still are, practiced side by side. I have decided that I don’t want to create single, syncretic rituals as part of my religious practice. If it won’t cause major issues, I would like to pursue each religion/pantheon in their own ways.

replies.title: Re: Concurrently Practicing Multiple Religions
replies.content:
1) The usual approach is to do it for the three nights leading up to the full moon, counting the night of the full moon (if it happens at night) or the one immediately before (if the full moon happens in the day) as the third.

2) No, the influence needs to be unfiltered. If you can, put it in an open window on a side of your home that gets plenty of moonlight, but where it'll be sheltered from the rain.

3) That seems extremely busy, but it's something a lot of people have done. At least one Roman emperor had a statue of Jesus on his personal altar, along with those of many other gods...

replies.title: Re: Concurrently Practicing Multiple Religions
replies.content:
If you aren't able to put the amulet in an open window as JMG suggests, remember that the Moon is shining on the tops of the clouds and that lunar influence is being absorbed by the water.

As an alternative, if what JMG suggests isn't possible, I would suggest that on the second or third night, you also put out a shallow bowl (ideally a silver bowl but a white crockery bowl would be fine) where the rain will fall into it and fill it at least partway. Get up before the sun rises and the moon sets, place the amulet into the bowl, cover it with a plate, take the bowl indoors, uncover it and set it on your altar or some dark or dimly lit place for awhile (maybe an hour) and let the amulet soak. Then dry it off and wrap it up until you are ready to consecrate it.

You can then do some other magic with the moon water if you wish. The magic should be compatible with the character of the amulet, obviously. If you don't have a use for the water, pour it out on a plant.

replies.title: Re: Concurrently Practicing Multiple Religions
replies.content: Nice! The rain magic is quite elegant.

replies.title: Re: yoga and chakras
replies.content:
Not JMG but I felt inclined to offer my comments.

As JMG has said a few times, the problem with East-West hybrids comes from the energetic anatomy of the subtle bodies and how these two traditions move energy in the body, as they do so differently. If you mess up with these energies badly enough people can get seriously injured, sicken and die. So, for example I wouldn't combine a routine that involves kundalini with magical traditions that use energy methods like the Middle Pillar. We don't know yet what causes this imbalance but it does happen.

That said, I wouldn't discard asana to be completely safe either, as they are meant to be done in particular sequences for specific purposes but I have found the general sequences that strengthen the whole body to be ok, but that is just my case. Pranayama is a different story, the only ones I've tried safely is deep breathing and the alternate nostril breathing that usually goes by the name nadi shuddhi as these also have balance and overall wellbeing as a goal. Things more complicated and specific than that I wouldn't mess with, with the exception of devotionals, while pursuing a western approach but I would say even that can create inconveniences if you are putting a lot of time into these things in some cases. Knowing what evolved from culture and tradition and what is more free-form from something that takes centuries to develop is quite tricky! And I am not convinced it is possible at all.

replies.title: Re: Abrahamic religions

There is a theme in Jewish religious thought that the Almighty has gradually reduced His immanence over time. According to the
Torah, God physically appeared to Moses. God accompanied the Israelites on their journey to the Promised Land in the form of a pillar of fire by night and smoke by day. replies.title: Re: Abrahamic religions
replies.content:
I'm Jewish by ancestry and upbringing (Reform variety), minimally observant, and have made some efforts to continue my Jewish education into adulthood. My understanding is that the closest thing to hypostases in Judaism of any stripe is the Shekinah, an immanent manifestation of Adonai, who is referred to in Hebrew (a language in which every noun has a gender) with female pronouns and adjectives. Adonai is masculine and is commonly referred to as Father and King. AFAIK, there is no implication that the Shekinah is a separate being from Adonai.

I agree with Robert Mathiesen's observation about cultural appropriation. Christianity, having originated as a Jewish sect, has a strong claim for the right to use the Hebrew Bible as a sacred scripture. Nonetheless, Christians assert that they understand the Tanach better than the people who wrote it, and their long history of interference in Jewish worship-- to the point of stealing and burning Torah scrolls and copies of the Talmud-- puts Christianity in the category of cultural appropriation. #NotAllChristians to be sure, but the Christian claims for themselves and against the Jews go back to the messy and hostile divorce between the two religions, and is a consistent theme in Christian attitudes and behavior up to the present.

replies.title: Re: Abrahamic religions
replies.content:
As someone who's had contact with all three Abrahamic religions asked about (I tried all of them before becoming a Druid), if I may:

1) No. They are very different deities.

2) No. Of the three that god actually felt the most active.

3) No. Another very different deity.

4) No. Another active deity.

5) Islam is full of people who take the same stances as Reformed and Conservative Judaism. The distinction is that the Jewish hardliners form separate denominations (Orthodox Judaism), rather than being mixed in with the people who are less serious.

comments.title: Odic force, follow-up
comments.content: This is a data point rather than a question. A few weeks ago I asked whether you knew anything about the effects of reflecting Odic force in a mirror, particularly a parabolic concentrator. Later I did find a reference to mirrors in the work of von Reichenbach; it was in his book of letters on the topic, or in an overview thereof, rather than in the magnum opus. Apparently such sensitive individuals as were exposed to Odic force in a mirror in his experiments, or according to their answers to his enquiries, experienced a disagreeable sensation from exposure to such reflected force, or to mirrors generally, rather than an agreeable sensation. Some could hardly bear to look in a mirror. I take this to mean that Odic force reflected in a mirror is likely to be of the unpleasant positively charged variety rather than of the pleasant negatively charged variety - viz., to appear red rather rather than blue to those sensitives who can see it. To me this suggests that concentrating Odic force with a parabolic mirror, or with a fresnel reflector, is probably not a good idea. So I won’t be doing it.

replies.title: Re: Odic force, follow-up
replies.content: Many thanks for this!  Worth knowing.

comments.title: Extreme emotions as a result of spiritual practice
comments.content:
I've had several false starts on spiritual paths. Usually I would practice daily for anywhere from several days to a few weeks and then one day I would find myself in a major crying jag that left me exhausted. Either that or I would begin to feel an inchoate dread that was also very tiring. After that I would drop the practice.

I've only recently come to understand why. My mother suffered from what I believe is now called emotional dysregulation, sudden bouts of extreme negative emotion in response to objectively minor difficulties. The possibility of ending up like her frankly terrifies me.

Is this a common experience at the beginning of a spiritual path? Are there established ways of dealing with it? Is there a way of telling when I can handle it myself and when I should look for the help of a teacher or guru?

replies.title: Re: Extreme emotions as a result of spiritual practice
replies.content: When that happens, in my experience, it usually means you have unresolved childhood traumas or emotional states, and those try to release once you begin turning your attention inward. Have you tried daily journaling to begin to unpack some of those emotional experiences? If not, might be worth a try.

comments.title: Skunks
comments.content: In response to Lady Cutekitten’s question last week about discouraging skunks from hanging around, it may be worthwhile to try and attract great horned owls (if that is doable). When reading nature guides for AODA work, I was blown away to learn that the greatest threat to skunks, after car tires, are great horned owls. They can lift the skunk off the ground which prevents the skunk from using the earth as a thrust-block for raising its tail and spraying. In the cemetery near our house, I have noticed that when we see great horned owls we don’t see skunks.

comments.title: Orgone and aluminum
comments.content:
You’ve described orgone devices, particularly orgone accumulators, as being composed of alternating layers of conductive and non-conductive materials. This is different from the description I have encountered online elsewhere. I think it was Poke Runyon who described these materials as being alternately organic and inorganic. But be that as it may, I am thinking of making some simple orgone devices.

I’ve made small orgone cushions in the past, from Poke’s instructions, using steel wool and felt. I would like to make an orgone cabinet and maybe a small orgone “shooter” - I think that’s the term. But steel wool has become rather hard to obtain, at least from my local hardware store, which now only sells it expensively, in tiny quantities. So I am thinking of substituting aluminum foil, as that is currently still abundant and cheap.

But aluminum is poison. In one online forum I have found it referred to negatively as a potential ingredient for making orgonite. Might I be creating DOR rather than orgone if I use it to make the aforementioned devices? I’ll be tracking down and checking with the appropriate online sources, but it might save me considerable time and trouble if you or someone else reading this happens to know the answer to my question.

replies.title: Re: Orgone and aluminum
replies.content: I don't happen to know that, but if you contact any of the groups currently doing research into orgone technology they can give you a detailed answer.

replies.title: Re: Orgone and aluminum
replies.content:
Hi Anonymous,

the people making a living from selling orgonite (eg Carol Croft) invariably use aluminium shavings and fibreglass resin (which is also toxic). I guess their sales speak to the effectiveness of those ingredients.

The salient thing, functionally speaking, seems to be the combination of metal/nonmetal = conductive/nonconductive = inorganic/organic.

scintillating iridescent elver

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Evening, JMG! Thanks once again for fielding our questions.

I have a few for you this week.

1) You've confirmed recently that you concur that psychedelic drugs damage the aura. In your experience, or from what you've heard, do different compounds cause substantially different effects?

2) Are these damaging effects mitigated when the substances are used in the context of a well-established spiritual tradition, under the guidance of a skilled practitioner? I'm thinking here of things like Native American peyote rituals, or Amazonian yage /ayahuasca use. The plants are regarded as powerful medicines and spirit allies in these traditions, and I've read reports of powerful healing experiences (of both physical and psychological illnesses).

Have these traditions developed ways to reduce the damaging effects of these substances? Or is it a question of a limited amount of damage being employed "surgically" to counteract a more pressing issue? Do you consider these reports of positive effects to be credible?

I could imagine a scenario in which use in cases of occasional illness is relatively benign, but repeated, long-term use is damaging. Yet spiritual professionals in these traditions apparently use the plants repeatedly, and acquire reputations for great spiritual powers and wisdom.

3) In the occult traditions with which you're familiar, does psychological trauma involve damage to the aura/etheric body?

Thank you again for taking the time to answer us!

- Fred N.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, the auric damage from different drugs appears to be different.

2) You'd have to ask practitioners of such traditions. I haven't studied them and therefore don't know.

3) It can, yes.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A more thorough answer:

1) Yes, different compounds have radically different effects. As a simple example, amphetamines have a powerful etheric effect but take a while for it to have a noticeable impact on the astral body. LSD has an immediate and profound impact on both bodies, DMT seems more astral than etheric. It's not unusual for sensitives who deal with a lot of junkies to figure out who's into what. It's like ingesting a poison, different ones cause different (generally bad) things to happen and harm different organs.

2) You are correct, the tradition usually features safeguards to prevent them from getting out of hand. How effective they are seems to vary from tradition to tradition. An (I think, Ohlone) shaman of my acquaintance is convinced the Ayahuasca users are irresponsibly insane. Some Persian Sufi rites actually feature the ingestion of explicitly toxic substances (like nightshade) which are then neutralized and eliminated through sophisticated internal alchemical techniques. The biggest consideration in resorting to these techniques is the context. These sorts of methods are only a small part of a much larger indigenous spiritual tradition, and probably shouldn't be used unless you have a connection either to the tribe or at least one of its elders/medicine men. There are plenty of other, equally effective tools for spiritual growth, and many of them are probably more accessible than peyote rituals if you are living in North America.
